title: Managing AI agents is not the same as being a manager
description: AI can absorb more execution work, but it still can’t replace the human lift, perspective, and collaborative challenge that make real teams valuable.
contentMarkdown:
I used to think that agent management would be identical to a human management skillset, but I was missing a core part of the process. It's easy to see why I got this wrong. When you work with agents, you spend more time assigning tasks, setting context, and reviewing outputs. You have to have a high rate of context switching and high quality of judgment. On the surface, that does look manager-like. 

But I think that framing misses the real shift. AI agents do not make you a manager, and the skills that make a good manager do not make a good “agent orchestrator” and vice versa. Instead, agents change the mix of work you spend your time on, regardless of if you're a manager or individual.

A useful way to think about this is to separate work into three buckets.

The first is tedious, repetitive work that requires very little judgment, mostly execution. The second is implementation and execution work that still requires judgment, but not fundamentally new ideas. The third is the process of learning, generating new ideas, and getting into different frames of mind. That third category is where a lot of the real value is now concentrating.

AI is already very good at the first category. It handles repetitive, mechanical work well. That is the obvious part.

It is also getting better at the second. It can help draft, refine, compare options, and accelerate work that still needs judgment but not fresh insight. That is why software work often feels less stressful with AI. A lot of mediocre but necessary work gets cheaper. It's easy to mistake the second category for progress, but as soon as this type of work gets cheap enough, it will be commoditized and no longer a differentiator. 

But the third category is different.

The most valuable work is often not just doing. It is learning, reframing, synthesizing, and discovering what actually matters. It is changing your mind in a useful way. It is getting to a better perspective.

And that is exactly where the manager analogy breaks.

Real management is not just delegation and review. The collaboration that managers do means working with other minds that bring something you do not have. Other people bring lift, perspective, resistance, energy, taste, and different ways of seeing the same problem. They challenge you in ways that are messy, inefficient, and often deeply valuable.

Agents do not do that in the same way.

They can be useful. They can push back a little (only if you instruct them to). They can suggest alternatives. But that is still different from a real teammate who brings lived context, independent judgment, and a genuinely different frame of mind. A real collaborator can surprise you. They can sharpen your thinking just by seeing the problem differently than you do.

That matters more now, not less.

As AI absorbs more repetitive work and compresses parts of implementation work, the remaining high-value work becomes more centered around judgment, learning, and perspective. In other words, the more useful AI becomes, the more important it is to protect the kinds of human collaboration that agents still cannot replicate.

That is why I do not think the right metaphor for these agent operators is “manager” per se. While learning to use agents effectively is an extremely valuable skill set and we will need workers that can execute with agents at a high level, I believe the role of manager will still persist beyond the point where everyone is an agent orchestrator because it is a unique set of skills. Maybe the ratio of workers to managers will decrease, or maybe it will stay the same and we've already reached a good equilibrium for productivity and collaboration with Agile. Either way, in a team context, the roles of agent-manager and manager are distinct. 

Whether your job title is manager or not, individuals should be using agents to reduce mechanical effort, while spending more of their energy on understanding the domain, making better decisions, and taking on more ambitious work. It also means staying open without becoming naive. You need skepticism and curiosity at the same time. Too much curiosity turns into hype. Too much skepticism means missing real leverage.

So no, AI agents do not make you a manager.

They make it easier to offload repetitive work and faster to move through parts of execution. But the most valuable work still depends on things agents do not bring in the same way: human lift, perspective, challenge, and collaboration.
